serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chana Chaat weighs approximately 250 g and represents a typical street-food or home-prepared serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Chana Chaat are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using cooked chickpea (kabuli chana) as the primary base, adjusted for the composite dish including onion, tomato, tamarind chutney, chaat masala, and sev. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chickpeas, cooked, boiled) for plausibility verification.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chana Chaat does not appear as a named entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 is estimated based on the published GI of boiled chickpeas (kabuli chana, GI ~28–33) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and Foster-Powell et al. 2002. The composite dish retains a low GI because chickpeas dominate the carbohydrate fraction; tamarind chutney and chaat masala contribute minimal additional carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 45.0) / 100 = 13.5 for a 250 g bowl (18.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 = 45.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from chaat masala and tamarind chutney
hypertension.guidance: Chana Chaat contains approximately 260 mg sodium per 100 g (650 mg per 250 g bowl), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit chaat masala and added salt, and benefit from the dish's potassium (250 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre content which support blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from chickpeas
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and 4 g dietary fibre per 100 g, Chana Chaat produces a low postprandial glycaemic response; a standard 250 g bowl yields a glycaemic load of approximately 13.5, making it a suitable snack for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when sodium and added sugar from chutneys are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, plant protein, and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chana Chaat provides 4 g dietary fibre and 6 g plant protein per 100 g with only 1.6 g saturated fat and 0 g trans fat, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the sodium content of 260 mg/100 g warrants moderation in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or heart failure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients and fibre from chickpeas and vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The chickpeas, tomatoes, and fresh coriander in Chana Chaat provide phytonutrients and dietary fibre that may support an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; no specific contraindications identified for rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chana Chaat is a popular Indian street food and snack dish made primarily from boiled kabuli chickpeas (white chickpeas) combined with finely chopped onions, tomatoes, and green chilies. The dish is seasoned with tangy tamarind chutney, spicy chaat masala, and fresh lemon juice, then garnished with chopped fresh coriander and crunchy sev (thin fried chickpea-flour noodles). The flavour profile is a characteristic Indian chaat combination of tangy, spicy, sweet, and savoury notes simultaneously. The texture contrasts soft chickpeas with crispy sev. Chana Chaat is widely consumed across India, particularly in North India, as an evening snack, street food, or party appetizer. It is naturally vegetarian and vegan. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 128 kcal, 6 g protein, 4 g fat, 18 g carbohydrate, and 4 g dietary fibre. The dish has a low glycaemic index of approximately 30 due to the resistant starch and fibre content of chickpeas, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of 260 mg per 100 g is moderate and primarily derived from chaat masala and tamarind chutney; individuals with hypertension should moderate added salt. Chickpeas are a good source of folate (120 mcg/100 g), iron (1.6 mg/100 g), and potassium (250 mg/100 g). The dish is low in saturated fat (1.6 g/100 g) and contains no trans fat, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed in moderation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using cooked chickpeas as the primary base for a composite dish. No single approved-source entry exists for Chana Chaat as a named composite dish; values represent a defensible adaptation. GI is estimated from published chickpea GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite dish preparation varies and no exact named entry was found in any approved source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chana Chaat as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite Chana Chaat preparation is not reported in the primary source consulted (ICMR-NIN). 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero trans fat for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked chickpeas. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: Chana Chaat is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing vitamin B12 or vitamin D; these are naturally zero for this vegan preparation. 5. Portions: the input contained duplicate entries for '1 serving (150g)'; deduplicated to one entry. The default serving used is 1 bowl (250 g) based on the grabfood source. 6. Cholesterol value of 6.0 mg/100 g from the input (sourced from grabfood) has been retained but treated with caution; chickpeas are plant-based and typically contain negligible cholesterol; this value may reflect minor contributions from sev (fried in oil) or cross-contamination in preparation. 7. GI glycaemic load per serving calculated for 250 g bowl: (30 × 45.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.5.
